title: Statement on IOSCO Compliance for Federal Reserve Bank of New York Administered Reference Rates

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York's Audit Group completed an independent review of the governance, quality, methodology, and accountability frameworks for seven reference rates it administers, including SOFR, EFFR, OBFR, TGCR, BGCR, SOFR Averages, and SOFR Index, finding all to be in compliance with IOSCO Principles for Financial Benchmarks.
key_points: New York Fed Audit Group independently reviewed the operational framework for seven reference rates: EFFR, OBFR, TGCR, BGCR, SOFR, SOFR Averages, and SOFR Index
Review covered four IOSCO principle categories: governance, quality of the benchmark, quality of the methodology, and accountability
All seven reference rates were determined to be in compliance with IOSCO Principles published in 2013 and endorsed by the Financial Stability Board
The compliance statement was released on July 17, 2025
New York Fed commits to annual compliance assessments going forward
IOSCO Principles were published in 2013 and endorsed by the Financial Stability Board as standards of best practice for benchmark administration
why_it_matters: SOFR and related overnight reference rates are foundational to trillions of dollars in financial contracts following LIBOR transition. This compliance confirmation verifies that the New York Fed's administration of these benchmarks meets international best practice standards for governance, methodology quality, and accountability. Users of SOFR-linked products (loans, derivatives, bonds) can have greater confidence in benchmark integrity.
market_structure_context: This document confirms the operational and governance integrity of key overnight reference rates (SOFR, EFFR, OBFR) that form the backbone of post-LIBOR market structure. These rates underpin the Fed's interest on reserve balances framework and serve as the primary risk-free rates for derivatives and loan markets. Compliance with IOSCO principles ensures accountability mechanisms are in place for benchmark administrators.
